name: Medieval Asturian-Leonese of Portugal

ancestors.notes: Medieval Leonese: the direct genetic ancestor, brought to Portucale during the Reconquista resettlement of the Trás-os-Montes region (9th–11th c. CE). This variety is the trunk from which Mirandese, Rionorese, and Guadramilese descend.

Galaico-Portuguese adstrate: the dominant Romance variety of the county and later kingdom of Portugal surrounding the Leonese-speaking enclaves of Trás-os-Montes. Contributed nasal vowels, lh/nh digraph convention, and diphthong contractions. The contact was asymmetric — Portuguese eventually became the prestige language.

Old Portuguese (Aviz-period) adstrate: as Portuguese consolidated as the state language, the Leonese-speaking communities of Miranda, Rionor, and Guadramil faced intensifying pressure. Portuguese orthographic conventions (lh, nh, ã) penetrated the writing tradition.
Gallaecian Celtic substrate of NW Iberia, shared through the Leonese parent. The Trás-os-Montes region has Celtic place-names (e.g. *Brigantia, castro toponyms).

Lusitanian substrate: the pre-Roman language of much of western Iberia, including the NE Portuguese highlands. Possible influence on /v/ ≠ /b/ preservation in this branch vs. the full betacism of central Asturian.
Mozarabic: Romance spoken by Christians under Moorish rule in the Leonese-reclaimed Douro corridor. Some Mozarabic-speaking settlers joined the Trás-os-Montes repopulation.

notes: Medieval Asturian-Leonese of Portugal (ast-PT-x-medieval), the Asturleonese variety that took root in the Terra de Miranda (Miranda do Douro), Rionor (Rio de Onor), and Guadramil areas of Trás-os-Montes following the Leonese resettlement of the Douro corridor (~9th–12th c. CE). This variety is the common ancestor of: (1) Mirandese / lhéngua mirandesa (mwl — still spoken, ~10,000 speakers), (2) Rionorese / mirandês de Rionor (nearly extinct), (3) Guadramilese / guadramilês (nearly extinct), and a notable influence on the Transmontano dialect of Northern Portuguese. KEY PHONOLOGICAL PROFILE: (1) 4-WAY SIBILANT CONTRAST (like parent Leonese and contemporary Galaico-Portuguese): voiceless apical /s̺/ ⟨ss, s-⟩ vs. voiced apical /z̺/ ⟨-s-⟩ vs. voiceless predorsal /t͡s/ ⟨ç, c-⟩ vs. voiced predorsal /d͡z/ ⟨z⟩. (2) INTERVOCALIC -l- AND -n- PRESERVED throughout the Leonese tradition ('salir', 'lhana') — the defining retention distinguishing the Mirandese group from all varieties of Portuguese. (3) LEONESE DIPHTHONGS retained: Lat. Ĕ → /ie/ ⟨ie⟩ (later written ⟨iê⟩ in Mirandese), Lat. Ŏ → /uo/ ⟨uo⟩ (later ⟨uô⟩). (4) PORTUGUESE NASAL VOWELS developing through Galaico-Portuguese contact: ⟨ã⟩ /ɐ̃/, ⟨on⟩ /õ/, ⟨an⟩ /ɐ̃/, etc. (5) HYBRID ORTHOGRAPHY: both Leonese ⟨ll/ñ⟩ and Portuguese ⟨lh/nh⟩ for /ʎ, ɲ/. (6) ⟨ch⟩ = /tʃ/ (Asturian/Leonese tradition, vs. Portuguese /ʃ/). (7) ⟨x⟩ = /ʃ/ (shared across the regional writing tradition). (8) PARTIAL BETACISM: /v/ survives as a distinct phoneme (Lusitanian substrate influence), unlike the full merger of central Asturian — this persists in modern Mirandese. (9) Latin F- PRESERVED as /f/ (no aspiration, unlike western Asturian). (10) NO VOWEL REDUCTION: 5-vowel quality system, unstressed vowels full (a defining Asturleonese feature vs. Portuguese ɨ/ɐ reduction). Sources: Leite de Vasconcellos (1882, 1900), Menéndez Pidal (1906), Vasconcellos (1900) *Estudos de philologia mirandesa*, Ferreira (1999) *Convenção Ortográfica*, Moisés Pires (2004), Martins (2011) *Leonese-Portuguese contact*.

sources.author: Penny, R.
sources.title: A History of the Spanish Language
